exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'жду + accusative' has no preposition; English 'wait' always takes 'for' before its object.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'wait you' is a direct calque of Russian 'жду тебя'. English 'wait' always needs 'for' before the person/thing. ✓ 'wait FOR you'.
✗ 'wait after' — wrong preposition. The fixed phrase is 'wait FOR': wait for you, wait for the bus, wait for me.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Two-word target: 'waiting for you'. The verb 'wait' always needs 'for' before its object — never drop it.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'слушать + accusative' has no preposition; English 'listen' always takes 'to' before its object.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'listen me' is a direct calque of Russian 'слушай меня'. English 'listen' always needs 'to' before what/who you're listening to. ✓ 'listen TO me'.
✗ 'listen on' — wrong preposition. The fixed phrase is 'listen TO': listen to me, listen to music, listen to the news.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Three-word target: 'Listen to me'. 'Listen' always needs 'to' before the object — no exceptions.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'зависит от' translates literally to 'depend from' — the fixed English preposition is always 'on', never 'from' or 'of'.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'depend from' — direct calque of Russian 'зависеть от'. English fixes the preposition: 'depend ON'. Always 'on'.
✗ 'depend of' is wrong. The fixed preposition is 'depend ON' — never 'of', never 'from'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'It depends on the weather'. 'Depend' always pairs with 'on' in English — the Russian 'от' does not transfer.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'принять решение' uses 'take' literally; English collocation is 'MAKE a decision' (British 'take a decision' exists but is rare and formal — for B1 use 'make').
correct_answers: I need to make a decision
I need to make a decision.
I have to make a decision
I have to make a decision.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'take a decision' is a direct calque of 'принять решение'. The standard English collocation is 'MAKE a decision'. (British formal sometimes uses 'take', but 'make' is the safe default.)
✗ 'do a decision' — wrong verb. English uses 'MAKE a decision', never 'do'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'make a decision' (not 'take' or 'do'). Russian 'принять решение' uses 'take', but English fixes this collocation with 'make'.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'делать домашнее задание' uses 'делать' (do/make); English collocation is 'DO homework', not 'make homework'.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'make homework' — Russian 'делать' splits into 'make' (create) vs 'do' (perform). For homework, the fixed verb is 'DO': 'do my homework', 'doing homework'.
✗ 'homeworks' — 'homework' is uncountable in English. No plural form: 'do my homework' (no -s).

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'I'm doing my homework'. Use 'DO homework' (not 'make'). Note: 'homework' is uncountable — never 'homeworks'.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'на прошлой неделе' uses 'на' (on); English uses NO preposition with 'last week' / 'last month' / 'last year'.
exercises.prompt_ru: Я ходила туда на прошлой неделе.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'on last week' is a direct calque of 'на прошлой неделе'. English uses NO preposition before 'last week/month/year': just 'last week', 'last Monday'.
✗ 'in last week' — also wrong. English uses NO preposition with 'last week': 'I went there LAST WEEK' (nothing before 'last').

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'last week' (no preposition). English drops the preposition entirely with last/next/this + week/month/year.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'на следующей неделе' uses 'на' (on); English uses NO preposition with 'next week' / 'next month' / 'next year'.
correct_answers: I will see you next week

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'on next week' is a direct calque of 'на следующей неделе'. English uses NO preposition: 'see you NEXT WEEK'.
✗ 'in next week' — wrong. English uses NO preposition with 'next week': just 'next week', 'next month'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'next week' (no preposition). Same rule as 'last week' — English drops the preposition with next/last/this + week/month/year.

exercises.calque_trap: Russian 'через неделю' translates as 'in a week' — English requires the indefinite article 'a' before the time period (Russian has no article). Common error: 'in week' (article drop).
correct_answers: We'll be back in a week
We will be back in a week
We'll be back in a week.

common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'in week' is missing the article. English requires 'a' before the time period: 'in A week', 'in A month', 'in AN hour'. Russian 'через неделю' has no article — English does.

common_errors.pattern_label: after a week (wrong prep for future)
common_errors.feedback: ✗ 'after a week' usually marks a past sequence ('after a week, he left'). For future ('through/in N time from now'), use 'in a week': 'We'll be back IN a week'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Target: 'in a week' (with 'a'). English needs the indefinite article before the time period — Russian doesn't, but English always does.
